coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as new eco-friendly and effective corrosion inhibitor
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Mingjun
Ren, Siming
Xue, Qunji
Zhao, Haichao
Wang, Liping
coredata.dc:description: Inspired by the high water solubility and low cytotoxicity of N doped carbon dots (CDs) prepared by using antibiotic aminosalicylic acid (ASA) as precursor, we anticipate that these CDs may possess an excellent protective ability to inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel in aggressive solutions owing to its special structure. Therefore, the investigation on the inhibiting effect of CDs as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel corrosion in 1 M HCl solution is first reported. Electrochemical techniques, weight loss, morphological and elements characterization are combined to investigate the inhibiting effect and corrosion mechanism. The results show that corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution is significantly inhibited by the addition of CDs, which is attributed to the formation of CDs adsorption film, and the adsorption of CDs obeys a Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Besides, the protection efficiency increases with increasing concentration of CDs and prolonged immersion time, and reaches equilibrium eventually.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Q235 carbon steel
Carbon dot
Eco-friendly inhibitor
Electrochemical measurements

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Applied Basic Research Key Project of Yunnan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by the National Key Basic Research Program (No. 2014CB643302 ) and “One Hundred Talented People” of the Chinese Academy of Sciences ( Y60707WR04 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of the CDs with ASA as precursor and photographs of CDs dispersed in ethanol (left) and 1 M HCl solution (right) with the concentration of 100 mg/mL.
(a) TEM, (b) SPM images of CDs and (b1-b3) height-profiles analysis along the corresponding lines in Fig. 2b.
XPS survey scans of ASA and CDs.
XPS high resolution survey scan of (a/b) C1s, (c/d) N1s and (e/f) O1s region of ASA and CDs.
(a) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, (b) Raman spectrum, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of ASA and CDs and (d) XRD pattern of CDs.
Potentiodynamic polarization plots for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentrations of the investigated CDs.

Nyquist (a) and bode (b and c) plots of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentrations of CDs after 30 min exposure and electrical equivalent circuit (d) used for fit the EIS results.
(a) Langmuir plot for CDs on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution and (b) corresponding inhibiting mechanism.
Variation of the corrosion rate (a) and inhibition efficiency (b) with different concentration of CDs after various immersion times.
Nyquist (a) and Bode (b and c) plots of Q235 carbon steel after various exposure times in 1 M HCl solution with 5 mg/L CDs and (d) used for fit the EIS results.
The surface roughness (Ra ) for the Q235 carbon steel after 88 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentration of CDs.

The SEM images of polished Q235 carbon steel (a/b) and the Q235 carbon steel after 88 h of immersion in blank 1 M HCl solution (c/d) and 1 M HCl solution with different concentration of CDs (1 mg/L CDs (e/f) and 5 mg/L CDs (g/h)).

The EDS spectra for the Q235 carbon steel after 88 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentration of CDs. (a) polished Q235 carbon steel, (b) blank 1 M HCl solution, (c) 1 M HCl solution with 1 mg/L CDs, (d) 1 M HCl solution with 5 mg/L CDs and (e) the variation on surface composition of Q235 carbon steel under different solutions.
The relative contents of C, N and O atoms for ASA and CDs.
Electrochemical polarization parameters for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl without and with different concentrations of the investigated CDs.
The fitting parameters for Q235 carbon steel after immersion in 1 M HCl without and with various concentrations of CDs.
The fitting parameters for Q235 carbon steel at various immersion times in 1 M HCl solution with 5 mg/L CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as new eco-friendly and effective corrosion inhibitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo, 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies
Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inspired by the high water solubility and low cytotoxicity of N doped carbon dots (CDs) prepared by using antibiotic aminosalicylic acid (ASA) as precursor, we anticipate that these CDs may possess an excellent protective ability to inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel in aggressive solutions owing to its special structure. Therefore, the investigation on the inhibiting effect of CDs as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel corrosion in 1 M HCl solution is first reported. Electrochemical techniques, weight loss, morphological and elements characterization are combined to investigate the inhibiting effect and corrosion mechanism. The results show that corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution is significantly inhibited by the addition of CDs, which is attributed to the formation of CDs adsorption film, and the adsorption of CDs obeys a Langmuir adsorption isotherm.
Besides, the protection efficiency increases with increasing concentration of CDs and prolonged immersion time, and reaches equilibrium eventually.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is commonly employed as structural material in engineering as well as daily life applications, such as petroleum industries, power plants, etc [1,2]. However, it is easily attacked and dissolved in hydrochloric acid solution when acid cleaning treatment is performed to remove of undesirable scales and rusts in several industrial sectors [3–5]. One way to protect the metal against corrosion is to add corrosion inhibitor that can adsorb on the surface forming a protective layer [6]. The acid corrosion inhibitors used commonly are organic compounds containing some electronegative atoms (such as N, S, P, O, etc.) and multiple bonds which have free electron pairs acting as a nucleophile to form a bond between the n-electrons and metal surface [1,6–10]. Therefore, when both these features exist in the inhibitors, the inhibition efficiency will increase in theory. Currently, the inhibiting effect of many organic compounds like triazoles [6,11,12], imidazoles [13–15], ionic liquids [4,16] and Schiff bases [1,2] have been reported. However, with increasing concern on environmental pollution and human health, the application of these organic inhibitors has been limited to some extent owing to their high toxicity [4]. Therefore, the development of facile methods to prepare the corrosion inhibitors of water-soluble, safe and non-toxic is highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs), one of the most significant classes of carbon-based fluorescent (FL) nanomaterials, have gained ever-increasing attention owing to their superior properties of high water solubility, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and unique photoluminescence (PL) properties [17–20]. Besides, it has been demonstrated that CDs have many potential applications, such as sensing, biomedicine, catalysis, and optoelectronic devices [19,21,22]. Lin et al. reported a facile method to prepare N doped CDs with low cytotoxicity and excellent multicolor cellular imaging capability by a single excitation wavelength [18]. Song et al. also reported a facile solvothermal process to prepare multicolor NCDs (with red, yellow, and green emission) from a popular antibiotic aminosalicylic acid (ASA) [17].

Here we can gain a key from the above N doped CDs that these CDs have a structure containing both electronegative atoms (such as N and O) and multiple bonds as well as they are in low cytotoxicity. It can be anticipated that these CDs may act eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors to inhibit the corrosion of metals. In present work, we prepare the N doped CDs according to previous report [17] and first report the corrosion inhibiting effect of CDs. The inhibition performance is evaluated by potentiodynamic polarization test, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), weight loss, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The results show that CDs have a good corrosion inhibiting effect on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution.

4-Aminosalicylic acid (ASA) was purchased from Aladdin. Ethanol and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 39%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 2 × 2 cm2 Q235 carbon steel was purchased from local suppliers. Deionized water (DI) was used during whole experiments.

The CDs were synthesized in a similar procedure reported by Song et al. [17]. ASA (0.7 g) was first dissolved in 70 mL of ethanol with ultrasonication, and then transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves. After the solutions were heated at 200 °C in oven for 18 h and cooled down to room temperature naturally, dark brown solutions were obtained. The crude product was purified with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off ∼ 3.0 kDa) for 1 day to remove the nonreactive molecules. The DI water would be replaced every 3 h. After removing solvents and further drying under vacuum, CDs were obtained as a black solid.

The Q235 carbon steel was abraded with SiC abrasive papers of 300, 800 and 1000 grit, respectively, degreased with alcohol and acetone, washed ultrasonically with DI, and finally dried at room temperature.

The corrosive medium was prepared by 1 M HCl solution without and with various concentrations (1, 5, 10, 50 and 100 mg/L) of CDs. The 1 M HCl solution was prepared by diluting analytical grade 36% HCl with DI. All tests were carried out at room temperature. A freshly prepared solution was used for each test.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was captured by Tecnai F20 microscope. Scanning probe microscope (SPM) measurements were performed with Veeco Dimension 3100V. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA DLD) was employed to examine the chemical composition and bonding status. The Raman spectrum was recorded with a Renishaw inVia Reflex excited by 532 nm laser. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Lambda 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was carried out with D8 ADVANCE.

Polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted in a CHI660E electrochemical station with a conventional three-electrode system. A platinum foil and saturated calomel (SCE) was used as counter and reference electrode, respectively. The Q235 carbon steel with the exposure area of 1 cm2 was the working electrode. All tests were conducted at room temperature. Before the test, open circuit potential test (OCP) was carried out for 30 min to reach a steady state. Then EIS was performed in a frequency range of 100 kHz–10 mHz at the OCP with 10 mV of disturbance signal. The EIS data were fitted and analyzed with Zsimpwin 3.21 software. Eventually, potentiodynamic polarization tests were conducted in the potential range of ±500 mV versus the OCP with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The same test was performed for 3 times to ensure the reliability.

Weight loss tests were carried out in a 20 mL vessel placed in a constant temperature thermostat. Samples with a dimension of 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.02 cm were immersed in 1 M HCl solution without and with various concentrations of the CDs. Then the samples were removed, washed with DI, dried and weighed at various time intervals. The average value of weight loss was used for calculations.

In order to measure the surface roughness of Q235 carbon steel sample after 88 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with various concentrations of CDs, surface profiler (Alpha-Step IQ) was employed. The average roughness of the sample was calculated with three times test.

The morphology and variation of surface composition for the Q235 carbon steel after 88 h immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with various concentrations of CDs were examined with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS).

XPS spectra were employed to characterize the chemical compositions and bonds on CDs. The full XPS spectra of ASA and CDs shown in Fig. 3 demonstrate that they have same elemental composition (i.e. C, N and O). The chemical compositions of ASA and CDs are summarized in Table 1 . It can be found that the content of O decrease from 20.3% to 12.6% after solvothermal reaction, indicating the decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions occur during the formation of CDs [18]. The C1s spectra in Fig. 4 a and b exhibit slight difference between the ASA and CDs, which show three types of carbon bonds such as CC (284.5 eV), CO/CN (285.6 eV) and CO/CN (288.5 eV) [21,23]. The characteristic peak at 284.5 eV corresponds to the C atom in graphitic structures. The decrease in the intensity of CO peak in CDs in comparison to that in ASA confirms that the decomposition of COOH group during the solvothermal process. For the N1s spectra in Fig. 4c and d, the peaks at 399.2 and 400.2 eV indicate that nitrogen exists mostly in the form of CNC (sp3) and (C)3 N (sp3), respectively [22,23]. These results prove that the synthesized CDs are nitrogen-doped CDs. The O1s peaks at 531.6 eV (530.8 eV) and 532.8 eV shown in Fig. 4e and f are associated with oxygen in the states of CO and COH/C O C, respectively [23,24].

The chemical structure of ASA and CDs was characterized by FT-IR. After the solvothermal treatment, significant changes occur for the structure of ASA. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the sharp absorption peaks at 3496 cm−1 and 3364 cm−1 for ASA are assigned to OH stretching vibration and NH stretching vibrations of amine groups, respectively [22]. The weak peaks between 2834 cm−1∼3007 cm−1 are attributed to the CH bond stretching vibrations [25]. The peak at 1648 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 corresponds to carbonyl CO vibrations and NH bending [26]. The characteristic stretch of the CN bond is at 1449 cm−1 and the stretching of CO occurs at 1295 cm−1 [18,27]. In case of the CDs, the peak at 3362 cm−1 is attributed to the unreacted OH and NH groups. The peak for CO in ASA disappears owing to the decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions. Besides, a new peak at 1617 cm−1 corresponds to NH2 deformation vibration and the peak at 1493 cm−1 is related to the CC vibrations. The Raman spectrum of CDs is further characterized with two obvious peaks: the D band (sp3-hybridized) at 1357 cm−1 and the G band (sp2-hybridized) at 1573 cm−1 (Fig. 5b), which is different with the ASA, owing to the occurrence of solvothermal reaction [17,27,28]. Fig. 5c exhibits the UV–vis absorption spectra of the ASA and CDs. It is clear the broad but weak absorption band (i.e., ≈400–550 nm) is due to the n-π* transitions of CN/CO and/or CN [17,19]. The XRD pattern of CDs in Fig. 5d shows that there is a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 24° with the interlayer spacing of 0.370 nm, which is broader than that of graphite (0.34 nm) [17,22]. This is attributed to the enrichment oxygen groups on the surface of CDs.

Potentiodynamic polarization plots for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentrations of CDs are shown in Fig. 6 . It can be observed that the polarization plots shift directly to negative potential with the increasing concentration of CDs. In particular, the cathodic current density decreases dramatically to a larger extent than the anodic current density. This can be attributed to the fact that the adsorbed inhibitor layers are formed on the surface of Q235 carbon steel, thus restricting the diffusion of oxygen from bulk solution to the cathodic sites of Q235 carbon steel substrate [4]. The electrochemical parameters, including corrosion potential (E
corr ) and corrosion current density (i
corr ) extracted from the potentiodynamic polarization plots, the degree of surface coverage (θ) as well as the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) are listed in Table 2 . The θ and η values are calculated according to the following equations:
(1)
θ
=
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
′
i
c o r r
0
(2)
η =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
′
i
c o r r
0
× 100
where
i
c o r r
0
and
i
c o r r
′
is the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of the inhibitor, respectively [29–33].

In the presence of the CDs, the E
corr values shift towards negative potential compared with the blank. Besides, a decrease in corrosion current density is observed when the inhibitor concentration is higher than 1 mg/L. It is worth mentioning that the η values also increase with increasing concentrations of CDs. Therefore, CDs can be deemed as effective corrosion inhibitors for Q235 carbon steel corrosion in HCl solution. For comparison, ASA (100 mg/L) has no inhibiting effect for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution (Fig. S1a and Table S1).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigate the behavior of the metal/solution interface in the absence and presence of CDs. The representative Nyquist (a) and Bode (b and c) plots of Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentrations of CDs are shown in Fig. 7 . It is obvious that all EIS plots exhibit the similar shape regardless of the absence and the presence of CDs, revealing that the addition of CDs does not change the electrochemical characteristics of the solution due to the relatively looser adsorption of CDs on Q235 carbon steel. The Nyquist plot shows a large capacitive loop at high frequencies (HF) and a small inductive loop at low frequencies (LF). The HF capacitive loop can be attributed to the charge transfer reaction, and the LF inductive loop may be attributed to the relaxation process like Cl− ads and H+ ads on the metal substrate [3]. In addition, the diameter of capacitive loops increases with the increasing concentration of CDs, suggesting the enhancement of inhibiting effect on the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel (Fig. 7a). Fig. 7b shows the Bode-impedance modulus plots. In general, the higher the impedance modulus at the lowest-frequency is, the higher corrosion inhibition of the samples is. It is clear form Fig. 7b that the impedance modulus at the lowest-frequency increases with the increase in the concentration of CDs. From Fig. 7c, we can find that the Bode-phase angle plot consists of a time constant and a portion of plot under the zero phase degree. Consequently, the electrical equivalent circuit in Fig. 7d is used to fit the EIS results and corresponding fitting parameters are summarized in Table 3 . In the electrical equivalent circuit, Rs is the solution resistance, R
p corresponds to the polarization resistance and CPE
p is a constant phase element used to instead of an ideal capacitor as a result of different physical phenomena like surface roughness, inhibitor adsorption, porous layer formation, etc. [34]. L is the inductance and R
L is corresponding resistance. The appearance of inductance L in the EIS result implies that the dissolution of Q235 carbon steel is still going on through the direct charge transfer in the presence of the inhibitor [35]. The data in Table 3 clearly shows that R
p value increases and CPE
p decreases with increasing concentration of CDs owing to the enhancement in the electrical double layer thickness [36], indicating that CDs can provide excellent corrosion protection to Q235 carbon steel exposed to 1 M HCl solution. However, when the concentration of CDs is too low, the corrosion inhibiting effect is not obvious. Similarly, the EIS for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution with 100 mg/L ASA is also obtained and corresponding results are presented in Figs. S1b–d and Table S2. In comparison with 100 mg/L CDs, we can infer that there is no inhibiting effect for ASA.

The adsorption isotherm can provide significant information about the interaction between the CDs and Q235 carbon steel surface. In general, there are two types of interaction to describe the adsorption behavior of the inhibitor molecule, which are chemisorption and physisorption [6]. The potentiodynamic polarization results in Table 2 are examined with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm according to Equation (3) and corresponding result is represented in Fig. 8 a.
(3)
C
i n h
θ
=
1
K
a d s
+
C
i n h
where
C
i n h
is the concentration of corrosion inhibitor and
K
a d s
is the adsorption equilibrium constant [5,37–39].

Fig. 8a presents the plot of C versus C/
θ
is a straight line with intercept of 1/K. According to Fig. 8a, it can be obtained that the value of K is 20619. A high
K
a d s
value implies that the CDs can be strongly adsorbed on the Q235 carbon steel surface6. The adsorption free energy (
Δ
G
a d s
0
) value of the CDs can be determined using the following equation [4,5,35]:
(4)
Δ
G
a d s
0
= − R T l n
( 55.5
K
a d s
)
where R is the molar gas constant and T is the absolute temperature.

In general, the
Δ
G
a d s
0
values can be used to understand the type of adsorption of corrosion inhibitor. When
Δ
G
a d s
0
value is −20 kJ mol−1 or more positive, there is an electrostatic interaction between inhibitor molecules and metal surface which is called as physical adsorption. While the value is −40 kJ mol−1 or more negative, transfer or sharing of electrons occurs from inhibitor molecules to metal surface and thus coordinate covalent bond forms, which is named as chemical adsorption [2,5,7,11]. The
Δ
G
a d s
0
for the investigated CDs is calculated as −34.6 kJ mol−1, which is between −20 and −40 kJ mol−1, indicating that the interaction between CDs and Q235 carbon steel surface involves both chemisorption and physisorption.

However, the nature of the metal, the chemical structure and characteristics of the corrosion inhibitor and charge distribution throughout the molecule has effect on the adsorption process. In this investigation, the detailed chemical structure of CDs is unknown owing to its preparation method while we can obtain some structure information of CDs according to the above IR, Raman, XPS and UV results. Therefore, based on both the experimental results in this investigation and theoretical results reported in previous investigation, we propose inhibition mechanism for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution with CDs as the inhibitors (Fig. 8b). Previous report stated that the surface of Q235 carbon steel was negatively charged owing to the absorption of anions (Cl−) in HCl solution, which would favor the adsorption of cations [6,37]. In HCl solution, CDs may exist in their protonated form in equilibrium with its corresponding molecular form.17 Therefore, the protonated positively charged form of CDs+ gets adsorbed on the negatively charged metal surface due to electrostatic interactions with the Cl−. In addition to physisorption, CDs may also adsorb on the surface of Q235 carbon steel through interactions between the heteroatoms with free electrons (N and O) in CDs and the unoccupied d orbital of the iron atoms [6,37,40–42]. At the same time, the electron from the d-orbital of iron atoms may be transferred to vacant π* (antibonding) orbital of CDs and thus strengthen adsorption on the carbon steel surface [11]. Therefore, the interaction between CDs and Q235 carbon steel surface involves a mix of chemisorption and physisorption due to structural properties of CDs, which is also supported by the result of
Δ
G
a d s
0
.

The effect of exposure time on the corrosion behavior of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentration of CDs is also investigated. To assess the stability of the inhibitor behavior on a time scale, the corrosion rate values (
W
c o r r
) and inhibition efficiency values (η
WL %) were obtained from weight loss measurements according to the following equations:
(5)
W
c o r r
=
Δ
m
m e a n
A t
(6)
η
W L
% =
W
c o r r
-
W
c o r r
'
W
c o r r
× 100
where
W
c o r r
and
W
c o r r
′
stands for the corrosion rates of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution without and with inhibitors, respectively.
Δ
m
m e a n
is the average weight loss of the Q235 carbon steel for three experiments. A is the surface area of the Q235 carbon steel and t is the immersion time [1,6,42].

Fig. 9 shows the variation of the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency with different concentration of CDs after various immersion times at room temperature. Results reveal the addition of CDs inhibits the corrosion of the Q235 carbon steel significantly. For the samples in 1 M HCl solution without and with 1 mg/L CDs, the corrosion rate decreases first then increase with prolonged immersion time. Inhibition efficiency is only 53% for CDs at 1 mg/L after 88 h of immersion. With the higher concentration of CDs, the corrosion rate decreases with prolonged immersion time and increasing concentration. And eventually, the inhibition efficiency is 93%, 94%, 95% and 96% for the Q235 carbon steel immersed in the 1 M HCl solution with 5, 10, 50 and 100 mg/L CDs after 88 h of immersion. This result indicates that the CDs can absorb on the surface of Q235 carbon steel surface to form a protective layer during the immersion process and the absorption reaches equilibrium at certain immersion time and concentration of CDs. Consequently, it can be found that the inhibition efficiency varies slightly after 88 h of immersion for all concentrations of CDs.

To better investigate the corrosive behavior of the Q235 carbon steel immersed in the 1 M HCl with CDs, the long-term EIS test is employed. Based on the above results, it can be found that the inhibition efficiency for the HCl solution with 5 mg/L CDs is higher than the 1 mg/L CDs, while it has little difference with further increasing concentration. Consequently, in this experiment, we choose the HCl solution with 5 mg/L CDs as object of EIS. Fig. 10 presents the EIS results for Q235 carbon steel after various exposure times in 1 M HCl solution with 5 mg/L CDs. It is obvious that Nyquist plots show different shapes at various immersion time (Fig. 10a). During the 0.5–10 h of immersion, the Nyquist plots show a large capacitive loop at high frequencies (HF) and a small inductive loop at low frequencies (LF). With immersion time elapsed (36–76 h), the inductive loop at low frequencies (LF) disappears and the Nyquist plots exhibit a large capacitive loop, indicating the dissolution rate of Q235 carbon steel becomes relatively slow due to the absorption of the inhibitor on the surface of Q235 carbon steel. In addition, the diameter of capacitive loops and impedance modulus at the lowest-frequency increase with the increasing immersion time, suggesting the enhancement of inhibiting effect on the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel. However, the diameter of capacitive loop decreases a little when the immersion time is up to 76 h. From Fig. 10c, it can be found that Bode-phase angle plot consists of a time constant and a portion of plot under the zero phase degree during 0.5–10 h but there is only one time constant during the immersion from 36 to 76 h. Therefore, the electrical equivalent circuit in Fig. 7d can only be used to fit the EIS results during 0.5–10 h. In case of the EIS results from 36 to 76 h, a new electrical equivalent circuit in Fig. 10d is proposed to fit and corresponding results are summarized in Table 4 . It is apparent that the R
p value increase with prolonged immersion time, which is attributed to the formation of adsorption layer on Q235 carbon steel by inhibitor molecules.

Based on the above results, the surface roughness (Ra ) for the Q235 carbon steel after 88 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentration of CDs is recorded with a 2D surface profiler, as given in Fig. 11 and Fig. S2. The Ra of Q235 carbon steel before immersed in 1 M HCl solution is about 90 nm. After immersed in 1 M HCl solution without and with 1 mg/L CDs, the Ra of the sample rises to 870 nm and 807 nm, respectively. When the concentration of CDs is higher than 1 mg/L, the Ra decreases dramatically and ranges slightly between 219 nm and 270 nm with increasing concentration of CDs (Fig. 11 and Fig. S2), suggesting the CDs can inhibit the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel by forming inhibitor film on the sample surface.

SEM images of the Q235 carbon steel before and after 88 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentration of CDs are also obtained, given in Fig. 12 and Fig. S3. It can be observed from Fig. 12a and b that the polished Q235 carbon steel surface is relative smooth with some polished scratches before immersion in 1 M HCl solution. In case of the Q235 carbon steel immersed in blank 1 M HCl solution (Fig. 12c–d) and 1 M HCl solution with 1 mg/L CDs (Fig. 12e–f), the Q235 carbon steel surface is highly corroded with the formation of cracks, pits and flakes. Obviously, the inhibiting effect is not obvious when the concentration of CDs in 1 M HCl solution is 1 mg/L. With increasing concentration of CDs (5 mg/L) in 1 M HCl solution, the corrosion of the surface of Q235 carbon steel seems light and there is less pitting with respect to those in blank 1 M HCl solution and 1 M HCl solution with 1 mg/L CDs (Fig. 12g). This observation clearly proves that 5 mg/L CDs exhibits good inhibition effect on the corrosion of the Q235 carbon steel. However, in high magnified SEM image (Fig. 12h), some regular peaks and gullies are present in the surface of Q235 carbon steel. Similar phenomenon can be observed for the Q235 steel substrate surface after immersion in 1 M HCl solution with higher concentration of CDs (Fig. S3). This indicates that the corrosion of the Q235 steel substrate reaches equilibrium when the concentration of CDs in 1 M HCl solution is high enough.

To investigate the variation on the surface composition of Q235 carbon steel after immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with different concentration of CDs, EDS micrographs and corresponding surface composition were recorded as given in Fig. 13 . Fig. 13a shows that the polished Q235 carbon steel before immersion in aggressive solutions mainly consists of C, O and Fe. For the Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution without (Fig. 13b) and with 1 mg/L CDs (Fig. 13c), the intensity of Fe peak decreases owing to the dissolution of Fe. At the same time, other peaks for minor elements (Cr, Cu, As, Al, P, Si, Ni) become obvious. In case of the Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution with 5 mg/L (Fig. 13d) and higher concentration (Fig. S4) of CDs, there is little difference for the EDS spectra with respect to the polished Q235 carbon steel, apart from the slight decrease in the intensity of Fe peak. To directly compare the variation on the surface composition, Fig. 13e gives the graph of the variation of content for different elements. It can be seen that the percentage atomic content of Fe and C for polished Q235 carbon steel is 73.74% and 23.02%, and those for Q235 carbon steel immersion in 1 M HCl solution without and with 1 mg/L CDs are 47.53%, 19.87% and 40.28%, 23.06%, respectively. This sharp decrease in percentage atomic content of Fe is attributed to the dissolution of Fe in HCl solution. However, with increasing concentration of CDs in 1 M HCl solution, the percentage atomic content of Fe decreases from 64.28% to 52.16%. At the same time, the percentage atomic content of C increases from 31.03% to 40.42% with increasing concentration of CDs. In combined with above results, it can be inferred that the decrease in the percentage atomic content of Fe is mainly attributed to the formation of a protective layer of inhibitors at the Q235 carbon steel surface by adsorption of CDs.

In summary, we reported the preparation of N doped CDs by a facile solvothermal method. Because of the unique structure and low toxicity, the as prepared CDs had great promise in corrosion inhibitors. Comprehensive and systematic characterizations were used to investigate the inhibitive effect and mechanism of CDs for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. Corresponding results could be summarized as follows:
(1)
The results showed that CDs had excellent inhibition efficiency for the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. In combination with results of potentiodynamic polarization plots and weight loss, it could be obtained that the inhibition efficiency increased with the increasing concentration of CDs and prolonged immersion time, and reached a maximum value (93–96%) when the absorption of CDs on the surface of Q235 carbon steel reaches equilibrium.
(2)
The adsorption of CDs on the Q235 carbon steel surface obeyed a Langmuir adsorption isotherm.
(3)
EIS plots exhibited a capacitive loop and an inductive loop at the initial immersion. The presence of inductive loop was attributed to the dissolution of the sample in 1 M HCl solution. With prolonged immersion, the inductive loop disappeared and the diameter of capacitive loop increased owing to the increase in absorption layer's thickness.
(4)
The analysis on surface roughness, SEM and EDS on Q235 carbon steel after 88 h immersion in 1 M HCl without and with various concentrations of CDs indicated that CDs could provide effective inhibiting effect via forming an efficient protective film on the Q235 carbon steel surface.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by the National Key Basic Research Program (No.2014CB643302) and “One Hundred Talented People” of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Y60707WR04).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2017.08.027.
